q:
Student: "Hello. I've come to ask about the English courses you run for international students."
Administrator: "Are you a student at the university?"
The student greeted and mentioned that she _______ to inquire about the English courses for international students. The administrator enquired if she was a student at the university.
options: had come
has come
have come
was coming
solution: In reported speech, 'have come' (Present Perfect) shifts to 'had come' (Past Perfect). 'She' replaces 'I' (first person to third).

q:
Shopkeeper: "What book do you want?"
Ramesh: "A reference book for Physics which I can use for my project work."
The shopkeeper asked Ramesh which book he wanted. Ramesh replied that _______.
options: he wanted a reference book for Physics which he could use for his project work.
he wants a reference book for Physics which he can use for his project work.
he had wanted a reference book for Physics which he could use for his project work.
he would want a reference book for Physics which he can use for his project work.
solution: 'Do you want?' → 'he wanted' (tense shifts to past); 'I' → 'he'; 'can' → 'could' (modal shifts to past). Question word order becomes statement order.

q:
City Planner: "The new park is a result of several months of research and planning."
Residents: "We don't know who is more excited – we or our children!"
The city planner said that the new park _______ of several months of research and planning.
options: was a result
is a result
has been a result
had been a result
solution: Direct speech 'is' (Simple Present) shifts to 'was' (Simple Past) in reported speech. 'A result' remains unchanged.

q:
Riya: "The book really caught my attention. It's called 'Eternal Echoes'."
Simran: "That sounds interesting! I'll definitely add it to my reading list."
Riya told Simran about the book that had captured her interest. Simran expressed that it _______.
options: sound interesting
sounded interesting
will be interesting
was found to be interesting
solution: Direct speech 'sounds' (Simple Present) shifts to 'sounded' (Simple Past) in reported speech.

q:
Teacher: "Today, we're going to learn about the importance of trees."
Student: "I pledge to plant some trees this weekend."
The student understood _______ and added that he _______.
options: the importance of trees / would plant some trees that weekend
the importance of trees / will plant some trees this weekend
importance of trees / would plant some trees that weekend
the importance of trees / had planted some trees that weekend
solution: 'I pledge' → 'he pledged/added that he would plant'; 'this weekend' → 'that weekend' (time shift in reported speech).

q:
Kriva and Manish had a conversation about the engagement of Kriva's brother. Manish asked: "How far is the engagement venue from your house?"
Report Manish's question.
options: Manish asked Kriva how far the engagement venue was from her house.
Manish asked Kriva how far is the engagement venue from her house.
Manish asked Kriva how far the engagement venue is from her house.
Manish asked Kriva how far the engagement venue has been from her house.
solution: Direct question 'How far is the venue from your house?' becomes indirect: 'how far the venue was from her house' — tense shifts, question mark removed, word order becomes statement form.

q:
Ankita: "Are we going to get some time off for sightseeing?"
Sharwari: "Oh yes! All the students will get two days for sightseeing."
Ankita asked Sharwari if they were going to get some time off for sightseeing, to which Sharwari replied in the affirmative and _______ two days for sightseeing.
options: that all the students should get
that all the students would get
that all the students ought to get
that all students must be getting
solution: 'Will get' (direct speech) shifts to 'would get' in reported speech (will → would in past reporting).

q:
Ankita: "Will you help me plan my itinerary?"
Tanu: "Yes, indeed! Where are you going?"
Ankita asked Tanu if _______.
options: she would help her plan her itinerary.
she will help her plan her itinerary.
she could help her plan her itinerary.
she would help her to planning her itinerary.
solution: 'Will you help me?' → 'if she would help her'. 'Will' → 'would'; 'you' → 'she'; 'me' → 'her'. Question becomes an embedded clause.

q:
Sunil: "Where is my car, sir? I parked it here."
Police Officer: "The car has been towed away as you parked it in the 'No Parking' zone."
The police officer replied that _______.
options: the car had been towed away as he had parked it in the 'No Parking' zone.
the car has been towed away as he parked it in the 'No Parking' zone.
the car was towed away as he parked it in the 'No Parking' zone.
the car had been towed away as he parked it in the 'No Parking' zone.
solution: 'Has been towed' → 'had been towed' (Past Perfect Passive); 'you parked' → 'he had parked' (tense and pronoun shifts).

q:
Sameer and Arun had a conversation. Arun asked: "What is the importance of planting trees?"
Report Arun's question.
options: Arun asked Sameer what the importance of planting trees was.
Arun asked Sameer what is the importance of planting trees.
Arun asked Sameer what the importance of planting trees is.
Arun asked Sameer what has been the importance of planting trees.
solution: 'What is the importance' → 'what the importance was'. Tense shifts from present to past; question word order becomes statement order.

q:
Mihika and Nitesh had a conversation about a shopping mall. Mihika asked: "Do you remember that cafe where we had a croissant?"
Report Mihika's question.
options: Mihika asked Nitesh if he remembered that cafe where they had had a croissant.
Mihika asked Nitesh if he remembers that cafe where they had a croissant.
Mihika asked Nitesh whether he remembered that cafe where they have had a croissant.
Mihika asked Nitesh that did he remember that cafe where they had a croissant.
solution: Yes/No question uses 'if/whether'; 'Do you remember' → 'if he remembered'; 'we had' → 'they had had' (past perfect); 'that' stays.

q:
Sameer: "At what time are we landing in Mumbai?"
Flight Attendant: "Sorry for the inconvenience, sir. The flight will be delayed by a few hours due to heavy rain."
The flight attendant replied that _______ due to heavy rains.
options: the flight would be delayed by a few hours
there would be a delay in flight landing
there will be a delay by a few hours
the flight could be delayed by a few hours
solution: 'Will be delayed' → 'would be delayed' (will → would in reported speech). The passive structure is preserved.

q:
Teacher: "Why have you not completed your homework?"
Student: "There was a long power cut in my locality and our inverter battery also ran out."
The student answered that _______.
options: there had been a long power cut in his locality and his inverter battery had also run out.
there was a long power cut in his locality and his inverter battery also ran out.
there had been a long power cut in his locality and his inverter battery also ran out.
there has been a long power cut in his locality and his inverter battery had also run out.
solution: 'There was' → 'there had been'; 'ran out' → 'had run out' (Simple Past → Past Perfect in reported speech). 'Our' → 'his'.

q:
Monika and Sunil had a conversation about summer vacation plans. Sunil asked: "Are you planning to visit your hometown during the summer vacation?"
Report Sunil's question.
options: Sunil asked Monika if she was planning to visit her hometown during the summer vacation.
Sunil asked Monika if she is planning to visit her hometown during the summer vacation.
Sunil asked Monika whether she had been planning to visit her hometown during the summer vacation.
Sunil asked Monika that was she planning to visit her hometown during the summer vacation.
solution: Yes/No question → 'if she was planning'; 'you' → 'she'; 'your' → 'her'; 'the summer vacation' stays as a known reference.

q:
Shambhavi: "How do you plan to contribute to the tree plantation drive?"
Akshat: "I have prepared a speech for the morning assembly."
Shambhavi asked Akshat _______.
options: how he had planned to contribute to the tree plantation drive
how did he plan to contribute to the tree plantation drive
how he planned to contribute to the tree plantation drive
how was he planning to contribute to the tree plantation drive
solution: 'How do you plan?' → 'how he planned'. The tense shifts to past; question word order becomes statement order (no inversion).

q:
Customer: "Where can I drop a request to get an exchange?"
Salesman: "Sir, you need to fill in this form before getting the exchange."
The salesman answered politely that _______.
options: he needed to fill in a form before getting the exchange.
he needs to fill in a form before getting the exchange.
he had needed to fill in this form before getting the exchange.
he would need to fill in a form before getting the exchange.
solution: 'You need' → 'he needed' (you → he; present → past); 'this form' → 'a form' (demonstrative pronoun shifts).

q:
Mahi and Pankaj had a conversation about volunteer work. Mahi asked: "How do you balance your volunteer work with your personal life?"
Report Mahi's question.
options: Mahi asked Pankaj how he balanced his volunteer work with his personal life.
Mahi asked Pankaj how did he balance his volunteer work with his personal life.
Mahi asked Pankaj how he had balanced his volunteer work with his personal life.
Mahi asked Pankaj how he balances his volunteer work with his personal life.
solution: 'How do you balance?' → 'how he balanced'; 'you' → 'he'; 'your' → 'his'; question word order becomes statement order.

q:
Rita: "Why did you decide to take up painting?"
Jeetu: "I view painting as a form of self-expression and relaxation."
Jeetu calmly explained that _______ self-expression and relaxation.
options: he views painting as a form of
he has viewed painting as a form of
he viewed painting as a form of
he is viewing painting as a form of
solution: 'I view' (Simple Present direct) → 'he viewed' (Simple Past in reported speech). 'I' → 'he'.

q:
Vendor: "It seems like you're in a hurry today!"
Customer: "Yes, I have to pick up my daughter at school soon."
The customer confirmed and explained that _______.
options: she had to pick up her daughter at school soon.
she has to pick up her daughter at school soon.
she would pick up her daughter at school soon.
she must pick up her daughter at school soon.
solution: 'I have to' → 'she had to' (have → had; I → she; my → her); 'soon' remains (time reference stays as context is clear).

q:
Raashi: "What is the most rewarding part of volunteering?"
Ratul: "Seeing the positive impact we can make in people's lives is the most rewarding part."
Ratul expressed that seeing the _______ is the most rewarding part.
options: positive impact we can make in people's lives
positive impact they can make in people's lives
positive impact they could make in people's lives
positive impact we could make in people's lives
solution: 'We can' → 'they could' — pronoun shifts from 'we' to 'they'; 'can' → 'could' (modal shifts to past in reported speech).

q:
Mother: "Ria, you went to the market. Is it crowded?"
Ria: "The market is always crowded with people."
Ria replied that _______.
options: the market was always crowded with people.
the market is always crowded with people.
the market had always been crowded with people.
the market has always been crowded with people.
solution: 'Is always crowded' (Simple Present) → 'was always crowded' (Simple Past) in reported speech. 'The market' stays unchanged.

q:
Priti: "Life's pretty dull. I need a job."
Meeru: "What kind of job are you interested in?"
In response to Priti's claim, Meeru _______.
options: asked what kind of job she was interested in.
asked what kind of job is she interested in.
asked what kind of job she is interested in.
asked about what kind of job had she been interested in.
solution: 'What kind of job are you interested in?' → 'asked what kind of job she was interested in'; 'you' → 'she'; 'are' → 'was'.

q:
Rachita shared information about the trek. Reena asked: "Was the trek difficult?"
Report Reena's question.
options: Reena asked Rachita if the trek had been difficult.
Reena asked Rachita if the trek was difficult.
Reena asked Rachita whether was the trek difficult.
Reena asked Rachita that the trek had been difficult.
solution: Yes/No question uses 'if/whether'; 'Was the trek difficult?' → 'if the trek had been difficult' (was → had been, Past Perfect).

q:
Postmaster: "Rama, show me a document to verify your identity."
Rama: "Sure, I will show you my driving licence."
Rama affirmed and said _______.
options: that was her driving licence.
this was her driving licence.
this is her driving licence.
that she would show him her driving licence.
solution: 'I will show you' → 'she would show him'; 'will' → 'would'; 'I' → 'she'; 'you' → 'him'; 'my' → 'her'.

q:
Alok: "Why did you get into a fight with Manu?"
Mohit: "Manu made some offensive allegations."
Mohit replied that _______.
options: Manu had made some offensive allegations.
Manu made some offensive allegations.
Manu has made some offensive allegations.
Manu was making some offensive allegations.
solution: 'Manu made' (Simple Past direct) → 'Manu had made' (Past Perfect in reported speech — past shifts one step further back).

q:
Rahul: "Father, I want a car."
Father: "No, you are not eighteen yet."
Rahul asked his father to get him a car but his father _______.
options: refused, saying he was not eighteen yet.
denied, saying he is not eighteen yet.
refused, saying you are not eighteen yet.
objected, saying he had not been eighteen yet.
solution: The father's negative response is reported with 'refused'. 'You are not eighteen' → 'he was not eighteen' (you → he; are → was).

q:
Rashid shared information about the piano. Tanu asked: "Who could have broken the piano?"
Report Tanu's question.
options: Tanu asked who could have broken the piano.
Tanu asked who can have broken the piano.
Tanu asked who could break the piano.
Tanu asked who has broken the piano.
solution: 'Who could have broken the piano?' — a wh-question with a modal. In reported speech: 'asked who could have broken the piano' (no tense shift needed for modal perfect 'could have').

q:
Postmaster: "Rama, who do you want to send the parcel to?"
Rama: "I want to send it to my uncle."
Rama replied _______.
options: that she wish to send to her uncle.
that she want to send to her uncle.
that she wanted to send to her uncle.
that she may send to her uncle.
solution: 'I want' → 'she wanted' (I → she; want → wanted in past reported speech). 'My uncle' → 'her uncle'.

q:
Cobbler: "Does this shoe hurt when you walk?"
Rohit: "Yes, my left shoe pinches my toes."
Rohit replied _______.
options: that his left shoe pinched his toes.
that his left shoe pinches his toes.
that his left shoe had pinched his toes.
that his left shoe was pinching his toes.
solution: 'My left shoe pinches' → 'his left shoe pinched' (my → his; Simple Present → Simple Past in reported speech).

q:
Coach: "Gautam, have you recovered from your muscle injury?"
Gautam: "After physiotherapy, I am feeling much better."
Gautam replied _______.
options: that after physiotherapy, he was feeling much better.
that after physiotherapy, he is feeling much better.
that after physiotherapy, he felt much better.
that after physiotherapy, he had been feeling much better.
solution: 'I am feeling' (Present Continuous) → 'he was feeling' (Past Continuous); 'I' → 'he' in reported speech.

q:
Ragini shared information. Jayanti asked: "Can anyone become a member of the library?"
Report Jayanti's question.
options: Jayanti asked if anyone could become a member of the library.
Jayanti asked if anyone can become a member of the library.
Jayanti asked that can anyone become a member of the library.
Jayanti asked if anyone had become a member of the library.
solution: Yes/No question uses 'if/whether'; 'Can anyone become?' → 'if anyone could become' ('can' → 'could' in reported speech).

q:
Clerk: "Where do you want to go?"
Passenger: "Please give me a ticket to Bhopal."
The passenger requested the clerk _______.
options: to give him a ticket to Bhopal.
to give me a ticket to Bhopal.
to give him a ticket for Bhopal.
that he should give him a ticket to Bhopal.
solution: An imperative/request 'Give me...' in reported speech becomes 'requested...to give him'. 'Me' → 'him'.

q:
Tony: "Why did you call me?"
Sarita: "Will you go to the museum with me?"
Sarita wanted to know whether Tony _______.
options: would go to the museum with her.
will go to the museum with her.
would go to the museum with me.
could go to the museum with her.
solution: 'Will you go?' → 'whether he would go'; 'will' → 'would'; 'you' → 'Tony/he'; 'me' → 'her' (Sarita is the speaker).

q:
Meera: "What did you buy?"
Father: "I bought a heater."
The father replied that he _______.
options: had bought a heater.
bought a heater.
has bought a heater.
would buy a heater.
solution: 'I bought' (Simple Past) → 'he had bought' (Past Perfect) in reported speech; 'I' → 'he'.

q:
Doctor: "Do you feel down from time-to-time, Mr. Gopalan?"
Patient: "Yes, I do not stay in a good mood."
The patient affirmed _______.
options: that he did not stay in a good mood.
that he does not stay in a good mood.
that he had not stayed in a good mood.
that he would not stay in a good mood.
solution: 'I do not stay' (Simple Present) → 'he did not stay' (Simple Past); 'I' → 'he'. Affirmative response is reported with 'affirmed that'.

q:
Grandson: "Grandpa, who are your superheroes?"
Grandpa: "Anyone who shows kindness and compassion to others."
Grandfather said that _______.
options: anyone who shows kindness and compassion to others is his superheroes.
anyone who showed kindness and compassion to others were his superheroes.
anyone who shows kindness and compassion to others was his superheroes.
anyone who had shown kindness and compassion to others is his superheroes.
solution: The grandfather's answer is a general/universal statement. Such statements may keep present tense in reported speech. 'My' → 'his'; 'are your' → 'are his'.

q:
Father: "Why ask so many questions, Latha?"
Latha: "I believe that if you don't know the answer, keep asking till you do!"
Latha exclaimed that in the event of not knowing the answer one should _______.
options: keep asking till one does.
kept asking till one does.
keep asking till one do.
kept on ask till one do.
solution: 'Keep asking' is retained in reported speech as a general principle/belief. 'You' → 'one' (generalized); 'you do' → 'one does' (third person singular).

q:
Rhea asked Deepak: "Have you finalised the magazine content to be submitted tomorrow?"
Report Rhea's question.
options: Rhea asked Deepak if he had finalised the magazine content to be submitted the next day.
Rhea asked Deepak if he has finalised the magazine content to be submitted tomorrow.
Rhea asked Deepak whether he finalised the magazine content to be submitted the next day.
Rhea asked Deepak that had he finalised the magazine content to be submitted the next day.
solution: Yes/No question uses 'if'; 'Have you finalised?' → 'if he had finalised' (Present Perfect → Past Perfect); 'tomorrow' → 'the next day'.

q:
Sana said to her team leader: "I'm sorry I forgot ma'am. I'll have the presentation ready for you very soon today."
Sana apologised for having forgotten and said _______.
options: that she would have the presentation ready very soon.
that she will have the presentation ready very soon.
that she had the presentation ready very soon.
that she would had the presentation ready very soon.
solution: 'I'll have' (will + have) → 'she would have'; 'I' → 'she'; 'today' may be dropped or changed to 'that day'.

q:
Teacher: "Did everyone understand the new assignment?"
Students: "Yes, we all got it, thank you!"
The teacher asked if everyone had understood the new assignment, to which the students _______, expressing their gratitude.
options: confirmed that they all had understood.
confirmed that they all have understood.
confirmed that they all understood.
confirmed that all of them will understand.
solution: The students' positive response ('Yes, we all got it') is reported as 'replied in the affirmative'. 'Got it' → 'had understood'.

q:
Sara: "Why did you choose to participate in this internship programme?"
Shabnam: "I am convinced this programme has the potential to enhance my abilities."
Sara asked Shabnam _______ in that internship programme.
options: why to choose to participate
to choose participation
why she had chosen to participate
either choice in participating
solution: 'Why did you choose?' → 'why she had chosen' (Simple Past → Past Perfect; 'you' → 'she'); statement word order used.

q:
Vendor: "It is nice to see you, Sir!"
Customer: "Yes, indeed! Unlike last month, I have been away for quite some time this month."
The customer explained that _______.
options: he had been away for quite some time that month.
he has been away for quite some time this month.
he was away for quite some time that month.
he would have been away for quite some time that month.
solution: 'I have been away' (Present Perfect) → 'he had been away' (Past Perfect); 'I' → 'he'; 'this month' → 'that month'.

q:
Sunil shared information about a holiday. Tariq asked: "Did you enjoy travelling by sea?"
Report Tariq's question.
options: Tariq asked Sunil if he had enjoyed travelling by sea.
Tariq asked Sunil if he enjoyed travelling by sea.
Tariq asked Sunil whether did he enjoy travelling by sea.
Tariq asked Sunil that had he enjoyed travelling by sea.
solution: Yes/No question uses 'if'; 'Did you enjoy?' → 'if he had enjoyed' (Simple Past → Past Perfect in reported speech); 'you' → 'he'.
